Program/Org Affiliation: Co-Director of Honors Program (and Honors Club)
Notes / Insights:
The program is active and committed to civic responsibility. As a requirement for all students in the Honors Program, community service projects provide direct benefits to the local community. Volunteer hours: Honors students must complete a minimum of 15 hours of community service per semester.Partnerships: The program collaborates with on-campus and off-campus affiliates and supports student-initiated projects.Community events: Past activities have included walks for causes such as AIDS, breast cancer, and Alzheimer's disease.Local support: Students volunteer for initiatives such as campus food pantries, youth groups, and local fundraisers
Through its ongoing service projects, the Honors Program provides the following benefits to the community: 
• Addresses local needs: Provides direct support to local initiatives like food pantries, addressing tangible issues in the community.
• Raises awareness:
 Students participate in fundraising events and walks for a variety of 
health-related causes, which helps raise community awareness and 
support.
• Cultivates civic engagement:
 By requiring mandatory volunteer hours, the program instills a strong 
sense of civic responsibility in its high-achieving students, 
encouraging lifelong community involvement.
Capabilities / Expertise: Civic Responsibility, Service Coordination, Awareness Raising, Civic Engagement

Notes / Insights: Leads a team that supports service learning and real world engagement

Program/Org Affiliation: Student Success Advocate and Immigrant Student Liaison
Notes / Insights:
The initiative and its student club, Grizzlies Beyond Borders,
 provide extensive resources for immigrant students, regardless of their
 immigration status (including undocumented, DACA, TPS, and asylum 
seekers). The services include: 
• Scholarship opportunities:
 The initiative provides information on and assistance with various 
grants and scholarships, such as the José Peralta New York State DREAM 
Act, the Dream.US Scholarship, and the institution-specific Cornelis and
 Angela Klein Scholarship.
• Campus resource connection:
 Immigrant liaisons help students navigate college life and connect with
 on-campus resources. The Grizzlies Beyond Borders club serves as a safe
 space for immigrant students and allies to find a supportive community.
• Career and financial guidance:
 The initiative offers career planning resources, advice on obtaining an
 Individual Taxpayer Identification Number (ITIN), and information on 
paid internships and fellowships available to students.
• Legal assistance:
 It connects students with CUNY Citizenship Now and other non-profit 
legal services to access free and confidential help for citizenship 
applications, DACA renewals, asylum claims, and other legal matters.
• Emergency grants:
 The college's Connect Center, which is closely affiliated with the 
initiative, provides emergency grants for eligible students to help with
 rent, utilities, medical bills, and other essential needs.
• Advocacy and community events:
 The initiative and student club organize community events and advocate 
for immigrant students, including protecting them from ICE enforcement 
actions on campus
Capabilities / Expertise: Scholarship Opportunities, Campus Resource Connection, Career and Financial Guidance, Legal Assistance, Emergency Grants, Advocacy
Communities Served: Undocumented Students, DACA Students, TPS Students, Asylum Seekers, Immigrant Students

Notes / Insights:
The United Men of Color (UMOC) program provides a supportive environment for men of color to 
succeed academically and personally. The organization was founded to 
address inequalities and empower underrepresented men. As part of the 
broader CUNY Black Male Initiative, UMOC offers services that include: 
• Academic support and mentoring: Members receive academic guidance to help them navigate their college experience.
• Workshops and personal development: The program holds workshops and activities that focus on personal and professional growth, well-being, and skill-building.
• Sociocultural dialogue:
 UMOC facilitates discussions where students can engage in dialogue to 
enhance their understanding of themselves and their communities.
• Community service projects: Participants take part in community service to build character and a sense of fellowship.
• Leadership development: UMOC helps scholars develop leadership skills that they can apply both in and out of school.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Personal Development, Leadership Development, Academic Support, Service Coordination

Notes / Insights:
The Social Justice Network (SJN) is a volunteer collective of staff, faculty, administrators and students at Bronx Community College who began meeting and organizing in the summer of 2020.

SJN worked together to develop the co-curricular programming we believe our communities needed in response to anti-Black racism in the US criminal justice system, health care, & education in addition to anti-Latinx/a/o policies and xenophobia.

We collaborate with established organizations and programs on campus (Womxn Up!, Unity and Strength; Association of Latino Faculty and Staff; ASAP; MEN; LGBTQI center, etc.), off campus and in our CUNY community.

SJN highlights and amplifies the work that is done surrounding Social Justice, Civic Engagement and Radical Wellness.

Program/Org Affiliation: Associate Director of College Now and oversees Bronx Civics Scholars Institute (BCSI)
Notes / Insights:
The Bronx Civics Scholars Institute is a six-week summer enrichment 
program for rising high school seniors. It has been active since at 
least 2006 and provides students with an academic and practical 
understanding of how local government works. The institute benefits the community by:
• Fostering civic engagement: Students engage with social justice issues through the lens of local, state, and federal laws.
• Providing real-world experience:
 Students earn college credit and complete internships at Bronx 
community-based organizations. Past partners have included The Bronx 
Defenders and the Northwest Bronx Community and Clergy Coalition.
• Developing future leaders: The program helps students acquire the skills and knowledge needed to be effective leaders and community advocates.
• Connecting youth with the Bronx: It teaches participants about the borough's history and connects them with advocates who are already creating positive change.
• Inspiring action:
 The summer program culminates in students delivering presentations 
about their work and research on issues facing the community, which are 
well-received by staff and community members.
Capabilities / Expertise: Leadership Development, Community Engagement, Internship Coordination, Presentation Delivery
Communities Served: Rising High School Seniors, Bronx Community, Staff and Community Members

Program/Org Affiliation: The CUNY Humanities Alliance Program Co-Director
Notes / Insights:
The CUNY Humanities Alliance focuses on increasing access to and 
engagement with the humanities, particularly for underrepresented 
students at CUNY's community colleges. Key contributions include:
• Graduate student training:
 The program trains CUNY doctoral students in effective teaching methods
 for diverse community college classrooms, preparing them for future 
academic careers.
• Undergraduate mentorship:
 The program provides robust mentorship and academic support to 
community college students, helping them successfully navigate their 
education and transfer to four-year institutions.
• Creating career pathways:
 It helps both graduate and undergraduate students envision and pursue 
career paths related to the humanities, emphasizing the value of 
humanistic study.
• Equity and inclusion:
 The initiative promotes a mission of social justice within higher 
education, working to make academic systems more equitable and inclusive
 for all students.
• Institutional collaboration:
 The alliance fosters collaboration and connection between the Graduate 
Center and several CUNY community colleges, including LaGuardia, Hostos,
 Guttman, and Borough of Manhattan Community College (BMCC).
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Training, Academic Support, Career Pathways, Equity and Inclusion
Communities Served: Underrepresented Students, Community College Students, Graduate Students

Program/Org Affiliation: Hostos Research Center (HRC) Research Programs Director
Notes / Insights:
The Hostos Research Center was established to be an innovative and collaborative community-focused endeavor. Its work serves students, faculty, and the wider South Bronx community by: 
• Promoting local research:
 The center fosters projects that address critical issues within the 
local community, bringing faculty together to collaborate on research 
that can generate solutions.
• Developing student skills:
 It creates research internships and other hands-on learning experiences
 to develop students' research skills and professional knowledge.
• Mentoring students:
 The center facilitates research mentorships between faculty and 
students, providing guidance and support for budding researchers.
• Supporting faculty:
 It helps faculty secure external funding and offers workshops and 
professional development opportunities in grant writing and research.
• Advancing educational practices:
 The center supports research on teaching and learning that helps 
improve educational practices, particularly those that are effective and
 have a high impact.
• Serving as a beacon of hope:
 By tackling the complex challenges of the South Bronx through academic 
and community-based research, the center aims to be a source of hope for
 the local area.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Professional Development, Grant Writing, Community Engagement, Academic Research

Notes / Insights:
The Mentor/Mentee Jobs on Campus initiative is still active and is part 
of the larger President's Initiatives, which are ongoing programs at the
 college. A recent announcement in August 2025 referenced new funding 
and mentioned the sustainability of the program. Contribution to the communityThe initiative benefits the Hostos community by providing valuable resources to both students and the wider campus, including: 
• Career readiness and professional development:
 It offers students paid, part-time on-campus jobs that provide 
real-world work experience and professional mentoring from faculty and 
staff.
• Financial support: The program offers a competitive hourly rate, which provides financial stability for students.
• Equity:
 It expands job opportunities beyond traditional work-study programs to 
include international students and those whose Federal Work-Study 
eligibility has ended.
• Retention: By fostering mentorship and providing support, the initiative contributes to increased student engagement and retention.
Capabilities / Expertise: Career Readiness, Professional Development, Financial Support, Equity, Retention

Notes / Insights:
CCF is a non-profit organization that helps justice-involved women earn college degrees and achieve career stability. Its work has multi-generational implications by reducing recidivism and empowering women to become leaders in their communities.
Specifically, CCF provides:

Higher education support: CCF assists formerly incarcerated women in achieving college degrees by helping them overcome barriers to education.
Leadership and career development: The program equips students with leadership skills and provides services that ensure career advancement after they complete their degrees.
Advocacy for policy change: CCF is actively involved in policy and advocacy work at the systemic level, addressing issues related to racial, gender, and economic justice. It has been a vocal proponent of campaigns such as "Ban the Box" and the restoration of Pell Grants for incarcerated individuals.
Wraparound support: CCF offers a network of peer mentoring, professional and college counseling, and other support services to promote long-term stability and success for its members.
Capabilities / Expertise: Higher Education Support, Leadership Development, Career Advancement, Advocacy for Policy Change
INI Alignments: Racial Justice, Gender Justice, Economic Justice

Program/Org Affiliation: the Office of Student Life Assistant Dean
Notes / Insights:
The Office of Student Life (OSL) serves both the internal college 
community and the local Bronx area. The office's work focuses on 
fostering student growth and maintaining a vibrant campus atmosphere. For the student community, the OSL:
• Encourages leadership and professional growth through extracurricular activities, workshops, and internships.
• Supports student organizations by assisting the Student Government Association and over 50 student clubs in managing administrative tasks and planning events.
• Promotes student wellness through counseling, orientation, and various wellness programs.
• Handles student grievances and ensures fairness through its role in the student judicial process and academic appeal reviews.
• Assists at-risk students by providing guidance and academic support to improve student retention.
• Builds a diverse, inclusive environment where students can interact with one another, faculty, and staff to exchange ideas. 
• For the local community, the OSL:
• Fosters campus-community interaction by encouraging student and faculty engagement with local community members.
• Sponsors campus events that reflect the diverse interests and backgrounds of the student body and neighborhood.
• Helps develop future leaders and employees from the local area by offering leadership activities, career exploration, and internships
Capabilities / Expertise: Leadership Development, Professional Growth, Student Organization Management, Wellness Program Administration, Grievance Handling, Academic Support, Campus-Community Engagement

Notes / Insights:
The SOS Team provides valuable support to students and the wider Hostos 
community by performing key volunteer services. The team's activities 
are robust and active, as evidenced by recent contract and event 
information. Services for students:
• Orientations and tours:
 Team members assist with new student orientations and college tours, 
helping incoming students navigate the campus and get settled.
• Registration assistance: They provide support to students during registration periods, ensuring a smoother process.
• Campus events: They are present at campus-wide activities, such as job fairs and other school-based events.
• Mentorship:
 The SOS Team acts as a feeder program for the Hostos Student 
Ambassadors, creating a tiered system for student leadership and 
providing mentorship opportunities. 
• Services for the broader community:
• Community service: SOS team members commit to performing community service hours as part of their program.
• Campus representation: By committing to one year of service, team members represent the college at various internal and external events.
• Local outreach: As volunteers, they participate in outreach activities that support the local community
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Registration Assistance, Campus Events Coordination, Volunteer Service Management

Program/Org Affiliation: Director of Center for Career Preparation and Partnerships (CCPP)
Notes / Insights:
the CCPP is still an active department at Guttman Community College. 
News articles published on the college's website in 2025 confirm the 
ongoing services and leadership of the CCPP team. The center offers a range of career readiness services, including:
• Job and internship opportunities: A Grizzly Jobs Portal is available to students.
• Career advising: The CCPP offers in-person and remote appointments with advisors.
• Workshops and panels: The center hosts career and industry panels with diverse professionals.
• Partnerships:
 The CCPP collaborates with other campus services, like the Career 
Innovation Hub, to provide students with workforce development and 
micro-credential opportunities.
Capabilities / Expertise: Career Readiness Services, Job Placement, Internship Opportunities, Career Advising, Workshop Facilitation, Partnership Building

Program/Org Affiliation: BronxWorks Director of Advancement and Communications
Notes / Insights:
BronxWorks aims to help individuals and families improve their economic 
and social well-being. It provides a wide range of services to thousands
 of Bronx residents, from toddlers to seniors. Its programs include: 
• Homelessness services: Providing emergency shelter, drop-in centers, and prevention services to help families and individuals avoid homelessness.
• Workforce development:
 Offering job training, placement services, financial counseling, and 
career guidance to help people become financially independent.
• Children and youth programs: Operating after-school and summer camp programs, college preparatory services, and literacy and arts enrichment activities.
• Senior services: Providing a variety of programs for older adults to help them lead active and independent lives.
• Health and wellness:
 Offering health education, access to fresh and healthy food through 
farm stands and food pantries, and programs for people with chronic 
illnesses.
• Immigration services: Assisting foreign-born residents with legal and citizenship applications.
• Legal services: Providing eviction prevention assistance and other legal help. 
• How volunteering helps college studentsCollege students who volunteer with https://bronxworks.org/ can gain valuable experience and develop skills while giving back to the community.
• Career exploration: Volunteering exposes students to different aspects of social services, potentially clarifying their career goals.
• Practical skills: It offers hands-on experience, allowing students to apply classroom learning to real-world situations.
• Networking: It provides opportunities to work alongside professionals and connect with other community members, building their network.
• Resume building: Community service is a positive addition to a resume, demonstrating a student's commitment to social responsibility.
• Academic credit: Some colleges or academic programs may offer credit for community service.
• Personal growth:
 It encourages students to engage with their environment and can help 
them better understand the challenges facing different communities.
Capabilities / Expertise: Career Guidance, Financial Counseling, Job Training, Legal Assistance, Practical Skills, Resume Building, Volunteer Coordination
Communities Served: Bronx Residents, College Students, General Public, Older Adults, People with Chronic Illnesses, Seniors

Program/Org Affiliation: Senior Director of Events & Partnerships at the The Door (Door of NY)
Notes / Insights:
Community contributions The
 Door uses a comprehensive, "whole-person" approach to provide services 
for New York City youth in a variety of areas. Its programs include: 
• Career and Education: High school equivalency and college preparation, job training, internships, and leadership programs.
• Counseling and Health: Health care, mental health counseling, and nutrition services.
• Legal Services: Free legal assistance for civil matters, including immigration, housing, and family law.
• Housing Support: Supportive housing and services for homeless and runaway youth.
• Arts and Recreation: Programs focused on creative expression and physical activity.
• LGBTQIA+ Services: Specialized programming, including health and gender-affirming services. 
• Benefits for college student volunteersCollege students can volunteer at The Door through its internship and pro-bono programs, particularly in its Legal Services Center. These opportunities provide hands-on experience and professional development. Benefits include: 
• Gaining professional experience:
 Internships, such as those for aspiring social justice lawyers, provide
 practical experience in fields like direct social work, law, education,
 and community organizing.
• Mentorship and networking:
 Interns and volunteers work with experienced staff and connect with 
legal professionals, judges, and activists through activities like brown
 bag lunch series.
• Skill development: Volunteers and interns can hone skills in interviewing, assessment, and legal work.
• Making a tangible impact:
 Volunteers directly contribute to addressing the urgent legal and 
social needs of young New Yorkers by providing counsel and advocacy.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Professional Development, Skill Development, Volunteer Coordination, Counseling, Health Services, Legal Assistance, Housing Support, Arts and Recreation, LGBTQIA+ Services
Communities Served: New York City Youth, College Students, Young New Yorkers

Program/Org Affiliation: OneGoal (New York)  Managing Director of Partnerships & Growth
Notes / Insights:
OneGoal works with schools in low-income communities to provide 
postsecondary advising and support to students. Their three-year 
program, which begins in a student's junior year of high school, is 
delivered as a credit-bearing class and focuses on: 
• Junior Year:
 Helps students develop their personal identity and professional 
aspirations, build academic skills, and create a list of potential 
postsecondary institutions.
• Senior Year: Assists students with postsecondary applications, financial aid, and preparing for obstacles during the transition.
• First Postsecondary Year: Provides continued support to students as they navigate their first year of college or other postsecondary paths. 
• How does it help students who volunteer?The program primarily serves students, or "Fellows," as they navigate the path to postsecondary education. However, https://www.onegoal.org/
 also offers volunteer opportunities for community members and mentors, 
which can provide benefits to student volunteers (Fellows) in several 
ways: 
• Mentorship:
 Fellows are matched with mentors for short-term or long-term 
activities, which offers individualized support for navigating their 
postsecondary path.
• Skill-Building:
 Mentors and volunteers can help Fellows strengthen skills crucial for 
their future, such as interviewing, resume writing, essay editing, and 
networking.
• Direct Assistance:
 Volunteers can provide hands-on help with the postsecondary application
 process, including financial aid applications like the FAFSA.
• Professional Connections:
 Interaction with community volunteers gives students opportunities to 
practice professional conversations, build their network, and receive 
feedback to improve their communication skills.
• Networking: Connecting with mentors and volunteers expands students' networks, which can lead to academic and career connections.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Skill-Building, Direct Assistance, Professional Connections, Networking

Program/Org Affiliation: Chief Executive Officer Bigs & Littles NYC Mentoring
Notes / Insights:
What Bigs & Littles NYC does for the community
• Provides one-on-one mentoring:
 The organization matches adult volunteers ("Bigs") with young people 
("Littles") aged 7 to 19. These pairs spend time together twice a month 
and stay in regular phone contact.
• Focuses on family support: Unlike other mentoring programs, https://www.google.com/viewer/place?mid=/g/1tzgkkdm&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwivj4rBuOWPAxWVk4kEHYKlEUwQqdYPegQIBhAD
 provides intensive support for the entire family. Its Program Managers,
 who are trained social workers, offer crisis counseling, workshops, and
 referrals to resources for housing, mental health, and other needs. 
There is also a "Mentoring Moms" program to provide support to mothers.
• Offers life skills workshops:
 The organization hosts workshops and events focusing on academic 
success, college access, career readiness, and emotional and physical 
wellness.
• Targets underserved communities:
 The program focuses on serving children and families in New York City's
 low-income neighborhoods, particularly in the Bronx, Brooklyn, and 
Queens. 
• How it helps college student volunteersVolunteering as a "Big" provides valuable personal and professional experience for college students:
• Gain experience with children:
 It offers experience in youth development and child well-being, which 
is beneficial for students in education, social work, and related 
fields.
• Develop professional skills:
 Students enhance skills in communication, leadership, and 
problem-solving through their mentorship and training. With constant 
support from professional Program Managers, they also learn about case 
management and family advocacy.
• Increase community awareness:
 By working with youth and families in underserved communities, 
volunteers gain a deeper understanding of the challenges and resilience 
of different populations in NYC.
• Expand network:
 The program offers opportunities to connect with other "Bigs" and 
staff, expanding the student's professional and social network.
• Fulfill a commitment to service:
 Students are able to give back to the community and make a lasting, 
positive impact on a young person's life by serving as a positive role 
model
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentoring, Family Support, Life Skills Workshops, Community Outreach
Communities Served: Underserved Communities, Low-Income Neighborhoods, Children and Families

Program/Org Affiliation: Interim Executive Director Bronx Community Foundation
Notes / Insights:
The Bronx Community Foundation's stated mission is to support and invest
 in community power to eradicate inequity and build sustainable futures 
for all Bronxites. It is the first and only community foundation 
dedicated solely to the borough. According to its public statements and website, the foundation engages in the following activities:
• Grantmaking:
 The foundation awards grants to Bronx-based nonprofits in focus areas 
such as community, health, economic security, and equity and justice. In
 late 2024, it awarded $150,000 to 15 local nonprofits and announced a 
new request for proposals.
• Community-driven initiatives:
 The BCF works with stakeholders from various sectors to identify 
problems and co-create solutions. Its participatory grantmaking model is
 designed to reflect the community's priorities.
• Addressing systemic challenges:
 The foundation focuses on long-term systemic change in areas like 
health, economic security, education, and digital equity. Recent 
announcements in 2025 have focused on digital literacy and broadband 
access.
• COVID-19 relief effort:
 In 2020, the BCF launched a major relief effort that raised and 
distributed over $10 million in resources to address urgent needs 
related to the pandemic.

Program/Org Affiliation: is the President and founder of the organization ImpactAbility and Image Impact International
Notes / Insights:
Based in the Bronx, New York, ImpactAbility and Image Impact International's mission is to fight for equal access to employment opportunities for college students with disabilities. They do this through several community-based programs and initiatives: 
• CareerReady!: An intergenerational mentoring and career readiness certificate program for college students and alumni with disabilities.
• Networking events: Organizing events to help students with disabilities connect with professionals.
• Disability Advocacy & Career Summit: A recurring event that focuses on disability advocacy, career development, and networking for students.
• Advocacy:
 The organization champions disability rights and celebrates Disability 
Pride and Independence days. It also sponsors College Students with 
Disabilities Advocacy Month each October. 
• The
 organization primarily serves students within the City University of 
New York (CUNY) system, focusing on providing support to a diverse group
 of first-generation students, veterans, and individuals from various 
backgrounds.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Career Readiness, Networking, Advocacy, Event Planning
Communities Served: College Students with Disabilities, First-Generation Students, Veterans, Individuals from Various Backgrounds

Program/Org Affiliation: Chief of Staff of the Center for an Urban Future (CUF)
Notes / Insights:
The Center for an Urban Future is currently active and influential in 
New York City policy. The organization's research has directly 
influenced legislation and initiatives, with recent notable impacts on 
job creation, higher education, and support for entrepreneurs. Some of CUF's community-focused work includes:
• Higher education:
 A 2022 CUF report led to the creation of CUNY Reconnect, a program that
 helps adults complete their degrees. The initiative was championed by 
the City Council and has already helped thousands of people earn 
degrees.
• Entrepreneurship:
 CUF's work on immigrant entrepreneurship helped drive new programs to 
support this growing sector. The organization's research also helped 
inspire NYC Boss Up, a $1 million initiative that provides grants to 
entrepreneurs living in New York City Housing Authority (NYCHA) 
apartments.
• Public spaces:
 CUF has campaigned for increased funding for public libraries, which it
 advocates for as important community anchors. Following its efforts, 
libraries received a significant budget boost in 2016.
• Targeted policy analysis:
 The organization produces accessible, fact-based reports that often 
provide actionable policy ideas to address challenges facing the city. 
These reports elevate critical, often overlooked, issues for 
policymakers and business leaders.
Communities Served: New York City Residents, Entrepreneurs, Adults, Immigrants, NYCHA Tenants

Program/Org Affiliation: Director of Student Life and Voter Registration Coordinator
Notes / Insights:
As Director of Student Life, Bennett and his team support civic engagement and community development through these activities: 
• CampusGroups management: They administer CampusGroups, a platform where students can find events, join clubs, and engage in campus activities.
• Voter registration and engagement:
 Through the Guttman Votes initiative, they encourage voter registration
 and active political participation, regularly holding on-campus 
registration drives.
• Partnerships and training:
 They arrange partnerships to provide students with training in 
leadership and pragmatic problem-solving, with the goal of encouraging 
students to become active community leaders.
• Promoting public service careers:
 They organize information sessions to introduce students and alumni to 
career opportunities in government and public service, such as those 
with NYC Service AmeriCorps.
Capabilities / Expertise: CampusGroups Management, Voter Registration and Engagement, Partnerships and Training, Promoting Public Service Careers

Program/Org Affiliation: Robert Pagan is the Senior Advisor for Partnerships, Alumni Engagement & Development
Notes / Insights:
Through its partnerships, the office supports the community by 
connecting students and employers for career-building initiatives. This 
work serves both the academic and wider community through several 
programs: 
• Internships and jobs:
 Facilitates internships and job placements, with resources available 
through the Center for Career Preparation & Partnerships (CCPP).
• Career readiness programs:
 Helps students acquire skills for professional development, including 
resume writing, interviewing, and general career exploration.
• Food and support services: The Connect Center
 collaborates with external partners to offer free resources to 
students, such as grocery deliveries and a food pantry, that address 
potential barriers to completing their education.
• Transfers and degree pathways:
 Forges academic partnerships with four-year colleges to create 
streamlined pathways for students to complete their bachelor's degrees.
• Professional attire:
 Has a partnership with the nonprofit Bottomless Closet, which provides 
students with free professional clothing for job interviews.

Program/Org Affiliation: The lead faculty member for the Guttman-Baruch Business Academy is Professor Sebastien Buttet
Notes / Insights:
The Business Academy benefits the community by providing a clear, 
supportive pathway for Guttman Community College students to earn a 
bachelor's degree at Baruch's Zicklin School of Business. It directly 
addresses the goal of social mobility for a diverse, largely minority 
and low-income, student population. Key community contributions include:
• Seamless transfer:
 The academy provides a streamlined and guaranteed admission process to a
 highly-regarded four-year CUNY college, provided students meet the 
academic criteria.
• Career readiness:
 Students gain access to professional development services, career 
coaching, workshops, and networking events at both Guttman and Baruch.
• Mentoring: The program fosters a social network and provides peer and alumni mentoring.
• Workforce development: By equipping students with in-demand business skills, the program helps build a skilled local workforce.
• Increased opportunities: It creates opportunities for a diverse student body that may not have otherwise pursued a bachelor's degree in business.
Capabilities / Expertise: Career Readiness, Mentoring, Workforce Development, Seamless Transfer

Program/Org Affiliation: CUNY EDGE Director at Guttman Community College
Notes / Insights:
CUNY EDGE (Educate, Develop, Graduate, and Employ) is a City University of New York (CUNY) program that helps students who receive public assistance stay on track to graduate. The program and its director, Tiffany Munoz, provide several services that directly benefit the college and the wider community.Services for students:
• Academic support: The program offers workshops on study skills and advisement to help students stay enrolled and improve their academic standing.
• Financial assistance: CUNY EDGE provides students with meal money, metro cards, and support for other financial needs.
• Career development:
 Students gain valuable professional experience through the HRA 
Fellowship Program, which places them in paid work opportunities on 
campus. 
• Community benefits:
• Workforce development:
 By helping students develop job skills and gain work experience, the 
program contributes to the local workforce and prepares graduates for 
meaningful employment.
• Increased access to education:
 CUNY EDGE works to ensure that students facing financial hardship have 
the support they need to pursue higher education, ultimately improving 
social mobility.
Capabilities / Expertise: Academic Support, Financial Assistance, Career Development, Workforce Development, Increased Access to Education

Program/Org Affiliation: Vice President of Enrollment Management & Student Affairs
Notes / Insights:
As the Vice President, Dr. Ambrose provides leadership and direction for
 the Division of Enrollment Management and Student Affairs (EMSA). His 
work is guided by the college's 2022-2028 Strategic Enrollment 
Management Plan and focuses on a number of key areas: 
• Recruitment and Enrollment:
 He oversees efforts to attract and enroll new students through 
initiatives like campus tours, orientations, and meetings with guidance 
counselors and parents.
• Student Support Services:
 He provides executive oversight for programs and services that help 
students succeed by addressing their academic, social, and personal 
needs.
• Student Retention and Success: He uses data to develop and implement programs aimed at increasing student retention, persistence, and graduation rates.
• Advocacy and Mentorship: Described as a mentor to students, Dr. Ambrose advocates for their success.
• Financial Resources:
 He works to provide students with crucial financial resources, such as 
scholarships and emergency grants, to help them complete their education
 with little to no debt
Capabilities / Expertise: Leadership, Mentorship, Advocacy, Program Development, Data Analysis

Notes / Insights:
The Guttman Honors Program actively contributes to the community, both on campus and beyond, by:

Program/Org Affiliation: Club Advisor for Guttman Media Group (GMG)
Notes / Insights:
GMG is a creative student club for aspiring photographers, 
videographers, and content creators. Its mission is to capture campus 
life and develop students' media production skills. For the Guttman Community College community, the group:
• Provides event coverage: GMG members attend and take pictures at campus events, providing visual content that documents college activities.
• Fosters a creative community: The group offers a space for students with a shared interest in visual storytelling to connect and collaborate.
• Develops student skills: Students can learn photography techniques, improve their videography, and sharpen their content creation abilities.
• Helps build student portfolios: The events and projects undertaken by GMG help members build a body of work for their professional portfolios.
Capabilities / Expertise: Content Creation, Photography, Videography, Media Production
Communities Served: Guttman Community College Students, Aspiring Photographers, Videographers, Content Creators

Notes / Insights:
The Peer Mentoring Program serves both the Guttman student body and the broader community in several ways:
• Assists new students:
 Peer leaders help ease the transition from high school to college by 
working with new students during the mandatory Bridge Program and in 
first-year learning communities.
• Provides academic support:
 Peer mentors hold academic support sessions called "Meet-Ups" and work 
with faculty to provide focused assistance in classrooms, supporting 
student academic achievement.
• Supports special populations:
 The program features different types of mentors, including Transfer 
Mentors and Wellness Ambassadors, who assist specific groups of 
students.
• Facilitates admissions events: Peer mentors assist with college admissions events by giving campus tours and welcoming prospective students.
• Engages in outreach:
 Peer mentors have worked with external partners to extend support 
beyond the campus. An example of this was a partnership with a local 
high school to cultivate academic and college success skills.
• Enhances student leadership:
 By participating in the program, student mentors develop leadership and
 professional skills that prepare them for future careers.
Capabilities / Expertise: Academic Support, Admissions Event Facilitation, Leadership Development, Mentorship, Outreach
Communities Served: Guttman Student Body, Broader Community, New Students, Transfer Students, Wellness Ambassadors, Prospective Students, Local High School Students

Program/Org Affiliation: managing Director, Strategic Partnerships and Innovation at The Bronx Defenders
Notes / Insights:
The Bronx Defenders also works to transform the system from the outside 
in. The Organizing Project empowers clients and community members to 
advocate for themselves and drive collective action. Their work 
includes: 
• Building grassroots power through events and forums.
• Lobbying elected officials for legislative reform.
• Offering "Know Your Rights" trainings to residents.
• Engaging in campaigns against discriminatory policing. 
• Systemic reform and advocacyThe organization uses its firsthand experience with the justice system to advocate for broader systemic change. 
• Policy advocacy: Experts work on legislative reform, media advocacy, and coalition-building to address system-wide injustices.
• Impact litigation:
 Attorneys file lawsuits to challenge systemic violations that affect 
large numbers of people, such as discriminatory policing and government 
seizure of property.
Capabilities / Expertise: Advocacy, Lobbying, Policy Advocacy, Impact Litigation

Notes / Insights:
As the largest arts education provider in the Bronx, DreamYard works 
with youth, families, and schools to create pathways toward equity and 
opportunity. The organization provides transformative arts and social 
justice education through programs offered during school, after school, 
weekends, and the summer. DreamYard's programs and community initiatives include: 
• In-School Residencies: DreamYard partners with over 40 public schools to offer arts residencies that align with NYC's arts and learning standards.
• After-School Programs: The DreamYard Art Center provides high-quality arts instruction and leadership development for K-12 students.
• BX Start: A center for gaming and entrepreneurship that offers free classes and events to bridge digital career readiness gaps.
• Legacy & Internships: Paid career readiness programs for high school and college students, helping them gain access to creative career paths.
• DreamYard Preparatory High School: The organization operates its own high school with a rigorous curriculum focused on arts and college preparation.
• Community Events:
 DreamYard hosts an annual student arts festival called DreamFest and 
offers other community programming, including a public park.
• COVID-19 Relief: In 2020, DreamYard helped launch the Bronx Community Relief Effort, which raised and distributed $15 million for the community.
Capabilities / Expertise: Program Development, Leadership Development, Career Readiness Programs, Community Programming
Communities Served: Youth, Families, Schools, K-12 Students, High School and College Students

Notes / Insights:
The Alliance's mission is to protect, restore, and improve the Bronx 
River corridor, making it a valuable ecological, recreational, and 
educational resource. Restoration and conservation
• Ecological restoration:
 The Alliance leads projects to restore native habitats, remove invasive
 plants, and improve wetlands. Their Conservation Crew is on the ground,
 removing channel blockages and reporting pollution like oil spills.
• Water quality monitoring:
 Programs like Project WATER DROP and Project WASTE involve volunteers 
in collecting data to track down and address sources of pollution, such 
as floatable trash and pathogens.
• Green infrastructure: It promotes solutions like permeable pavement and green roofs to manage stormwater runoff, which often pollutes the river.Community involvement:
 A major part of the Alliance's work is engaging community members and 
volunteers in clean-up efforts, water quality monitoring, and habitat 
restoration.Educational resources:
 They provide resources and programs for local students through the 
Bronx River Living Laboratory, turning the river into an outdoor 
classroom.Environmental justice:
 The Alliance is committed to environmental justice, ensuring that 
planning processes are transparent and that development along the river 
benefits all communities, especially those that have historically 
received fewer resources.Developing the Greenway:
 The Alliance works to develop and rehabilitate the network of parks and
 trails along the eight miles of the river that run through the Bronx, 
converting old industrial sites into public green spaces.Connecting residents to the waterfront:
 The organization creates opportunities for local residents to connect 
with nature through events, educational programs, and guided canoe 
trips.
Capabilities / Expertise: Conservation, Restoration, Water Quality Monitoring, Green Infrastructure, Community Engagement, Education, Environmental Justice

Program/Org Affiliation: the Senior Director of Development and Operations at Rocking the Boat
Notes / Insights:
Based in the South Bronx, Rocking the Boat
 is an active youth development program that offers young people 
hands-on experiences in boatbuilding, environmental science, and sailing. Its contributions to the community include: 
• Youth development and empowerment:
 The programs help young people develop self-confidence, teamwork, and 
critical thinking skills. This hands-on learning empowers participants 
to set ambitious goals for their future.
• Educational achievement: Participants receive academic and social-emotional support. Rocking the Boat
 reports impressive success rates: 94% of its students graduate high 
school, compared to 57% in Hunts Point, and 92% go on to enroll in 
college or a trade school.
• Environmental restoration:
 Students actively restore the Bronx River by collecting scientific 
data, monitoring water quality, and removing invasive plant species. 
They also build floating wetlands to filter the water, leading to 
improved biological diversity.
• Accessible nature and recreation:
 The organization hosts free Community Rowing events on the Bronx River 
on Saturdays throughout the summer. These events introduce community 
members to the river and educate them about its history and ecology.
• Community revitalization: By involving students and the public in restoring their local waterway, Rocking the Boat
 helps to change the perception of the Bronx River from a polluted area 
to a natural resource to be proud of. This revitalizes the community and
 offers residents a unique connection to nature.
Civic Domains: Youth Development, Environmental Science, Sailing, Education, Community Revitalization
Capabilities / Expertise: Program Development and Operations, Youth Empowerment, Academic Support, Social-Emotional Support, Environmental Restoration, Community Engagement
Communities Served: Young People, Students, Community Members, Residents of the South Bronx

Program/Org Affiliation: Senior Director of Development and Communications
Notes / Insights:
New Settlement Apartments has been working to revitalize the Mount Eden area of the southwest Bronx since 1989 by providing affordable housing and a wide range of community services. Their primary goal is to empower residents to improve their choices related to education, employment, housing, wellness, and creative expression.
Key community programs include:

Education and Youth Services: New Settlement
offers extensive school and after-school programs, a College Access Center, a Program for Girls and Young Women, and the YouthBuild program. They partner with Community School District 9 to provide academic and enrichment resources for thousands of students.
Housing and Advocacy: Initially transforming abandoned buildings into affordable housing, New Settlement
continues to develop and manage affordable homes. It also runs a Community Action for Safe Apartments (CASA) initiative to help tenants.
Wellness and Recreation: The New Settlement Community Center
features a swimming pool, dance studios, and a green roof terrace. It offers a variety of affordable programs for all ages, including yoga, dance, and swim classes. The center's food pantry serves hundreds of residents each month.
Community Organizing: The Parent Action Committee (PAC) advocates for educational improvements within District 9, and broader community organizing efforts encourage resident leadership and involvement in local issues.
Workforce Development: New Settlement's Young Adult Opportunity Initiative assists young adults with job prospects and professional development.
Civic Domains: Community Development, Housing, Education, Youth Services
Capabilities / Expertise: Program Development, Advocacy, Community Organizing, Workforce Development
Communities Served: Low-Income Individuals, Residents of the Southwest Bronx, Students, Young Adults

Program/Org Affiliation: Hope Burgess is the President & CEO of Banana Kelly Community Improvement Association
Notes / Insights:
BKCIA primarily serves the Longwood, Hunts Point, Mott Haven, and 
Morrisania neighborhoods of the South Bronx and remains active through a
 range of housing and social service programs. Its community work 
includes: 
• Affordable housing:
 As a community development corporation, BKCIA develops and manages over
 65 rental buildings and more than 2,240 housing units, ensuring they 
remain affordable for area residents.
• Community organizing:
 The organization's Resident Council empowers community members to 
advocate for housing issues, and its organizing team engages with tenant
 associations and neighborhood groups.
• Rental assistance:
 BKCIA provides rental assistance programs for residents, including 
formerly homeless individuals and families with disabilities.
• Case management:
 Case management services assist residents with retaining their 
tenancies, finding job placement, and accessing supportive services.
• Food justice:
 Through its Healthy Communities Initiative, BKCIA organizes and 
supports community gardens and holds regular food giveaway events in 
partnership with the New York Common Pantry.
• Supportive services:
 The organization offers Medicaid service coordination for families with
 disabled members and has also historically offered youth education 
programs, such as after-school and summer camp.
Capabilities / Expertise: Affordable Housing Development, Community Organizing, Rental Assistance, Case Management, Food Justice, Supportive Services
Communities Served: Low-Income Individuals and Families, Formerly Homeless Individuals and Families with Disabilities, Youth

Program/Org Affiliation: The Point CDC's Director of Community Development
Notes / Insights:
The Point CDC uses arts, environmental justice, and youth development to
 serve the Hunts Point section of the South Bronx. Its programs and 
services include: 
• Youth development:
 Offers free programs such as arts, digital media, photography, and 
circus training for hundreds of young people annually. This programming 
is designed to support both social development and leadership skills.
• Arts and culture:
 Provides arts education and a platform for local artists to showcase 
their talents. The organization operates a visual arts gallery and a 
flexible performance space, making arts and culture accessible to 
residents.
• Community development and environmental justice:
 Undertakes projects focused on environmental justice and 
sustainability. Past initiatives include the creation of Hunts Point 
Riverside Park and advocacy for green spaces. The organization also runs
 a free community WiFi network, an electric shuttle service, and a 
Community Shared Solar project to help lower-income households.
Capabilities / Expertise: Program Development, Service Coordination, Leadership Development, Arts Education, Platform Creation, Community Engagement, Advocacy, Sustainability
Communities Served: Low-Income Households, Young People, Local Artists, Residents of Hunts Point

Program/Org Affiliation: the Director of Development and External Affairs
Notes / Insights:
the BCA has served as a cultural hub for the borough, focusing on 
supporting local artists and providing arts programming to the 
community. It has a particular focus on artists from historically 
marginalized communities, such as artists of color, women, and LGBTQ+ 
individuals. Its programs can be categorized into three main areas:1. Connector Programs: These initiatives directly link Bronx arts and culture with the community. 
• Artist Residencies:
 The BCA places professional artists at community sites to provide 
creative experiences for residents, including seniors and other 
vulnerable populations.
• Longwood Arts Project:
 This flagship program includes the Longwood Art Gallery, which focuses 
on artists from underrepresented traditions and cultural practices.
• Bronx Memoir Project:
 This writing project provides workshops for Bronx residents to share 
their personal stories, culminating in an annual anthology. 
• 2. Resource Programs: The BCA provides funding and professional development for artists and cultural organizations. 
• Grantmaking:
 It offers multiple grant programs, such as the Community Engagement 
Grants (CEG), to fund local arts and culture projects across various 
disciplines.
• Bronx Recognizes Its Own (BRIO) Awards: This program provides monetary awards to foster artistic excellence in local artists.
• Workshops and Training:
 The BCA offers professional development workshops and technical 
services to help artists and arts organizations build their careers and 
practices. 
• 3. Advocacy Programs:
 The organization acts as a pioneering advocate for cultural equity and 
works to advance support for the arts at all levels of government. 
• Cultural Assets Map (CAM) and History Map (CHM): These programs help document and advocate for the borough's cultural landscape.
• Strategic partnerships:
 BCA partners with other organizations to ensure artists' needs are 
represented and that equitable practices are implemented across the 
cultural sector.
Capabilities / Expertise: Artist Residencies, Grantmaking, Workshops and Training, Advocacy
Communities Served: Local Artists, Cultural Organizations, Historically Marginalized Communities (e.g. artists of color, women, LGBTQ+ individuals), Bronx Residents, Seniors and Vulnerable Populations
INI Alignments: Cultural Equity, Advocacy for the Arts

Program/Org Affiliation: the Director of Education and Public Engagement at the Bronx Museum of the Arts
Notes / Insights:
Community impact and partnershipsThe
 Bronx Museum uses art and education to serve its community through 
various programs and initiatives. Its partnerships support its mission 
of providing free, dynamic programming and opportunities. School and education partnerships
• School Partnerships:
 The museum forms deep collaborations with local public schools to 
provide art education, curricula, and visits for students. This serves 
schools that may have little or no arts funding.
• Teen Programs:
 The Teen Council and Teen Summer programs offer young people 
opportunities to engage with contemporary art, curate exhibits, create 
art, and develop collaborative skills. Teens are paid a stipend for 
their participation. 
• Artist partnerships
• AIM Fellowship:
 A free career accelerator program for promising New York City-based 
artists, with a focus on equity. It provides professional development 
training, mentorship, and opportunities for early-career artists. 
• Exhibitions and free access
• Free Admission: All exhibitions and programs at the https://www.google.com/viewer/place?mid=/m/03wdjzl&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwi8343i8uyPAxWMk4kEHSY9B94QqdYPegQIDBAC are 100% free, which is a core part of its mission to ensure accessibility to the contemporary art world.
• Diverse Representation:
 The museum champions BIPOC (Black, Indigenous, and people of color) 
artists, featuring work that reflects the borough's diverse communities 
and cultural tapestry.
Capabilities / Expertise: Art Education, Curriculum Development, Program Management, Mentorship, Professional Development Training, Career Acceleration
Communities Served: Local Public Schools, Teenagers, New York City-based Artists, BIPOC (Black, Indigenous, and people of color) Artists, Diverse Communities

Program/Org Affiliation: the current Vice President of SoBRO
Notes / Insights:
SoBRO addresses all aspects of community development to create a 
thriving and prosperous South Bronx. It works through a variety of 
programs focused on business, housing, education, and career 
development. Business services:
• Provides technical assistance to small business owners and entrepreneurs.
• Helps
 businesses secure financing, apply for certification as a Minority or 
Women-Owned Business Enterprise (MWBE), and navigate government 
agencies.
• Offers support for commercial revitalization to attract and retain businesses in the Bronx. 
• Housing and real estate development:
• Develops and manages affordable and supportive housing units for low- and middle-income families and individuals.
• Constructs and rehabilitates commercial and industrial spaces, often converting vacant properties into productive areas. 
• Youth education and development:
• Runs
 after-school programs that combine academic support with skill-building
 in areas like music, video production, and journalism.
• Operates
 the YouthBuild pre-apprenticeship program, which helps young adults 
aged 17–24 earn their high school equivalency diploma and gain job 
credentials.
• Offers the Summer Youth Employment Program (SYEP) to provide paid opportunities for career exploration and skill-building. 
• Adult education and career development:
• Offers adult education classes, including English language, civics, and basic literacy.
• Provides job training and placement services to prepare residents for employment and help them achieve self-sufficiency.
Capabilities / Expertise: Technical Assistance, Financing Support, Certification Navigation, Commercial Revitalization, Affordable Housing Development, Real Estate Development, Youth Education, Pre-Apprenticeship Program, Summer Youth Employment Program, Adult Education, Job Training, Placement Services
Communities Served: Low-Income Families, Middle-Income Families, Individuals, Young Adults Aged 17-24, Residents

Program/Org Affiliation: The Bronx Chamber of Commerce Foundation (BxCCF) President and CEO
Notes / Insights:
The foundation focuses on economic opportunity, equity, and inclusive 
growth throughout the Bronx. It serves a wide range of organizations, 
from small businesses to nonprofits, through initiatives that include: 
• Business support:
 Provides education, training, mentorship, and leadership development 
for small and mid-sized businesses and community-based organizations.
• Community connections: Acts as a liaison between community members and city government, encouraging participation in local Community Boards.
• Targeted assistance:
 Offers resources for Minority- and Women-Owned Businesses (MWBEs) and 
helps businesses recover from crises, such as COVID-19, through the NYC 
Small Business Resource Network.
• Youth and career development: Facilitates career development programs and internships to prepare Bronx students and professionals.
• Economic advocacy: Works with public and private partners to influence policy and advance economic development that benefits the entire borough.
• Sponsor programs:
 Runs an "Adopt a Member" program where current members can sponsor 
one-year memberships for other small Bronx businesses, expanding 
networking opportunities.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Leadership Development, Education, Training, Resource Provision, Policy Advocacy
Communities Served: Small and Mid-Sized Businesses, Community-Based Organizations, Minority- and Women-Owned Businesses (MWBEs), Youth, Professionals, Bronx Students

Program/Org Affiliation: the Program Coordinator for the BronxWorks Center for Achieving Future Education (CAFE)
Notes / Insights:
The CAFE program is an active BronxWorks initiative that helps young 
people and their families transition from high school to higher 
education. The program offers a range of services at no cost to assist 
students with their post-secondary plans. For high school students, the program offers the following services: 
• College and trade school assistance: One-on-one counseling and workshops guide students through the entire application process.
• Financial aid and scholarships:
 The program assists with completing financial aid applications like the
 FAFSA and NY Tap, in addition to helping students find and apply for 
scholarships.
• SAT prep: Free SAT preparation is offered for high school juniors in partnership with New York Cares.
• College tours: Students can visit colleges and attend college fairs to learn more about their options. 
• For middle school students, the CAFE program helps ensure a smooth transition to high school.
Capabilities / Expertise: College and Trade School Assistance, Financial Aid and Scholarships, SAT Prep, College Tours
Communities Served: High School Students, Middle School Students, Families

Program/Org Affiliation: the Chief Administrative Officer for the Bronx County Historical Society (BCHS)
Notes / Insights:
The Bronx County Historical Society promotes local pride and community engagement through the preservation and promotion of Bronx history. Its activities include: 
• Operating museums and a research center. It runs the https://www.google.com/viewer/place?mid=/m/03wj0v5&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwi6x--r_-yPAxWXElkFHT9dAeYQqdYPegQIBhAC at the https://www.google.com/viewer/place?mid=/m/03wj0v5&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwi6x--r_-yPAxWXElkFHT9dAeYQqdYPegQIBhAE and the https://www.google.com/viewer/place?mid=/m/03whtl7&sa=X&ved=2ahUKEwi6x--r_-yPAxWXElkFHT9dAeYQqdYPegQIBhAG. It also operates a Research Center, which houses a Research Library and the Bronx County Archives for public use.
• Education and public programming.
 The Society offers educational programs, lectures, and cultural events 
for the public and schools. These programs range from guided tours and 
workshops to the annual Bronx High School valedictorian awards ceremony.
• Preserving and sharing historical records.
 It collects and digitizes historical artifacts, documents, and other 
materials related to the Bronx. The Society makes this information 
available to researchers, students, and the general public through its 
archives and digital platforms.
• Conducting historical projects.
 The Society leads and participates in research initiatives like the 
Bronx African American History Project and the Bronx Latino History 
Project, which use oral history to document the borough's diverse 
communities.
• Publishing materials. The BCHS is a major publisher of books and articles about the Bronx and produces The Bronx County Historical Society Journal, the oldest continuously published historical periodical in the metropolitan New York area.
Capabilities / Expertise: Education and Public Programming, Preserving and Sharing Historical Records, Conducting Historical Projects, Publishing Materials

Notes / Insights:
Nos Quedamos
 is dedicated to making the South Bronx a resilient and empowered 
community through planning and policies focused on affordable housing, 
open space, environmental justice, and community renewal. Development and affordable housingThe
 organization was founded to prevent resident displacement during

officials for funding and programming, conducts civic engagement 
initiatives like voter registration, and empowers youth through 
volunteer and employment programs, including the Summer Youth Employment
 Program

Program/Org Affiliation: President and founder of the MarbleJam Center for Creative Arts & Enrichment
Notes / Insights:
The MarbleJam Center provides an inclusive space for individuals of all 
ages with special needs, such as those with autism spectrum disorders 
and developmental disabilities. Services
• Creative Arts Therapies:
 The center offers individual and group therapies using creative arts 
modalities like music, art, and dance to help participants express 
themselves, build confidence, and manage sensory challenges.
• Educational and Enrichment Programs:
 They provide adaptive art classes, social skills programs, and summer 
workshops in a variety of fields, including digital and fine arts, 
drama, and film.
• Skill Development:
 The center focuses on helping participants achieve milestones that can 
enhance their independence and quality of life, including communication,
 socialization, and self-regulation.
• Community Events:
 MarbleJam hosts events, such as its annual SummerJam festival, that are
 open to the wider community. These events feature art, music, games, 
and other activities to showcase its clients' work and foster a 
supportive environment.
• Economic Contribution:
 The center sells handcrafted art created by its students to help fund 
its programs. This provides recognition for the artists' work while also
 making a positive financial impact on the organization.
Capabilities / Expertise: Therapy, Education, Enrichment, Skill Development, Event Planning, Economic Empowerment
Communities Served: Individuals with Special Needs, Families of Individuals with Special Needs

Notes / Insights:
The Adolescent Mentoring Program works to strengthen the lives of young 
people who are involved in the juvenile justice system by offering 
support and life skills. 
• Mentorship and advocacy: It pairs participants with highly trained mentors who act as positive role models to help them set and achieve personal goals.
• Skill building: The program provides tutoring, assistance with homework, college preparation, and life skills training.
• Academic support: The program aims to keep participants on track to graduate and move on to advanced education or training.
• Community integration: Mentors also work to foster positive connections between participants and their teachers, peers, and family members.
• Prevention:
 By offering guidance and support, the program helps young people make 
better decisions and reduce their risk of deeper involvement with the 
justice system.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship and Advocacy, Skill Building, Academic Support, Community Integration, Prevention

Program/Org Affiliation: Executive Director of Community Connections for Youth (CCFY)
Notes / Insights:
Community Connections for Youth is a Bronx-based non-profit that works to end mass youth 
incarceration by disrupting the "youth prison pipeline." The 
organization provides effective, community-driven alternatives to 
incarceration for young people in the South Bronx. It is centered on the
 belief that strengthening a young person's connection to their 
community is the most effective way to help them avoid deeper 
involvement in the justice system. Key community contributions include:
• Mentoring and support: Offering mentoring and general support to system-involved youth and their caregivers.
• Family and peer support:
 Providing a Parent Support Program where trained parent coaches—who 
have navigated the justice system themselves—help guide families of 
system-involved youth.
• Alternatives to incarceration:
 Developing and supporting effective, community-led programs for 
court-involved youth. For example, the South Bronx Community Connections
 program connects young people to positive adults and activities as an 
alternative to detention.
• Capacity building:
 Training grassroots faith and neighborhood organizations to help them 
develop and improve their own youth programming. The Youth Justice 
Capacity Challenge, for instance, provides grants and training to local 
organizations.
• Systemic change: Advocating for systemic juvenile justice reform by training and consulting with policymakers and juvenile justice agencies.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentoring and Support, Family and Peer Support, Alternatives to Incarceration, Capacity Building, Systemic Change
Communities Served: Youth-Impacted Individuals, Caregivers, Faith and Neighborhood Organizations

Notes / Insights:
Pregones/PRTT is a vital cultural and civic institution that champions 
Puerto Rican/Latinx cultural heritage. It achieves its mission through a
 variety of programs and initiatives that benefit the community in the 
following ways: Creating and presenting culturally relevant art
• Performance: It creates and stages original musical theater and plays rooted in Puerto Rican/Latinx culture.
• Representation:
 It centralizes perspectives often missing from public discourse, 
challenging disparaging narratives and promoting authentic and dignified
 representations of Latinx communities.
• Artistic Exchange: It partners with other artists to present their work, fostering a broad exchange of ideas and talent. 
• Empowering artists and community members
• Artist Residencies:
 The "Step Up: Artists in Residence" program provides emerging and 
early-career artists with resources, mentorship, and a stipend to 
develop and showcase their work.
• Mentorship: The organization is dedicated to mentoring future generations of artists and leaders within the field.
• Workshops and training: It operates a training unit for young actors and offers workshops and master classes to the public. 
• Promoting health and well-being
• Health Initiatives:
 The "Abrazo / Embrace" initiative uses interactive theater to raise 
awareness about mental health, particularly in post-pandemic 
communities. It has provided resources and referrals to thousands of New
 Yorkers.
• Community Wellness: Projects like the play BURNED are co-developed with community members to address local issues in a sensitive and empowering way. 
• Fostering civic engagement and solidarity
• Community Building: As a place-keeping institution, Pregones/PRTT has built a lasting presence in the South Bronx and Hell's Kitchen, becoming a hub for community, history, and belonging.
• Activism and Advocacy:
 It uses its platform to address social inequities and stands in 
solidarity with marginalized communities, including African American 
communities.
• Cultural Preservation:
 The theater plays a pivotal role in bringing awareness to Puerto Rican 
and Latinx cultures, showcasing how they are integral to the unique 
cultural fabric of the United States.
Capabilities / Expertise: Civic Engagement Facilitation, Mentorship, Training and Capacity Building, Health Education and Awareness, Advocacy
Communities Served: Puerto Rican/Latinx Communities, African American Communities, General Public

Program/Org Affiliation: Business Development Manager at The HOPE Program
Notes / Insights:
The HOPE Program has been active for 40 years, serving New Yorkers with 
high unemployment rates by helping them find jobs and achieve financial 
stability. The program's core services for the community include: 
• Job readiness and workforce training:
 HOPE provides training programs to equip individuals with the skills 
needed for employment. In the Bronx, it offers a seven-week program with
 a $50 weekly stipend.
• Job placement assistance:
 It helps graduates with their job search, resume and cover letter 
development, interview preparation, and placement into full-time 
employment.
• Career advancement: For graduates, HOPE provides ongoing support after job placement, known as the "HOPE for life" motto.
• Digital access and technology training: HOPE provides access to essential technology and skills to overcome the digital divide.
• Holistic support:
 The program offers comprehensive "wraparound services" to address 
different barriers to employment, including mental health counseling, 
case management, and access to clothing, meals, and transportation.
• Green job training: The organization also provides training and employment opportunities in emerging economies, such as the green jobs sector.
• Reducing inequity:
 HOPE aims to address systemic barriers like racism in hiring, health 
disparities, and involvement with the criminal justice system.
Civic Domains: Economic, Workforce, Labor, Employment, Finance, Business
Capabilities / Expertise: Job Readiness Training, Workforce Development, Job Placement Assistance, Career Advancement Support, Digital Access and Technology Training, Holistic Support Services, Green Job Training
Communities Served: New Yorkers with High Unemployment Rates, Individuals

Notes / Insights:
The Osborne Association is a non-profit organization that provides 
services to individuals, families, and communities impacted by the 
criminal justice system. They offer opportunities for people to "heal 
from and repair harm, restore their lives, and thrive" by challenging 
systems rooted in racism and retribution. Programs for the communityThe
 Osborne Association has a wide range of programs that serve individuals
 and families at various stages of contact with the justice system: 
• Court services:
 These services provide alternatives to incarceration for people who 
have been arrested, helping to reduce reliance on jail and prison.
• Reentry services:
 The organization supports formerly incarcerated people with 
transitional housing, job placement, and assistance with health and 
wellness needs.
• Housing:
 The Osborne Association offers transitional and permanent supportive 
housing to help those returning to the community find stable and 
welcoming places to live.
• Family services:
 Specialized services for families of incarcerated individuals, 
including programs for children and mentoring for young adults with 
incarcerated parents, aim to keep families connected.
• Advocacy:
 The organization advocates for policy changes to reduce incarceration, 
improve prison conditions, and expand access to housing and other 
services for justice-involved individuals.
• Gun violence prevention:
 In the Bronx, the Osborne Association runs the Bronx Osborne Gun 
Accountability and Prevention (BOGAP) program, which addresses the root 
causes of gun possession.
Capabilities / Expertise: Court Services, Reentry Services, Housing, Family Services, Advocacy, Gun Violence Prevention
Communities Served: Justice-Impacted Individuals, Families of Incarcerated Individuals, Children, Young Adults with Incarcerated Parents

 University NYC uses hands-on beekeeping and environmental stewardship 
to educate and empower urban communities, with programs specifically 
designed for young people and students. For the community:
• Environmental advocacy:
 The organization promotes environmental justice by advocating for green
 spaces in underserved communities, tackling issues like climate change,
 and strengthening community resilience.
• Hands-on workshops:
 Bee-U hosts interactive hive experiences, honey tastings, and beeswax 
workshops for community members of all ages to learn about beekeeping 
and the environment.
• Community events:
 They host free and low-cost events, such as the annual Opening Day in 
Maker Park, to educate the public on bees and environmental issues. 
• For college students and young people (ages 16–24):
• Urban Beekeeping Fellowship:
 This paid, hands-on program trains young people in beekeeping, 
leadership, and environmental advocacy. It includes lessons on 
environmental justice and potential career paths in the green economy.
• Workforce development:
 The organization provides graduates with a "Green Jobs Pipeline," 
offering pathways to employment through connections with partner 
organizations, apprenticeships with the New York Bee Club, and 
opportunities to be hired as program coordinators.
• Targeted support:
 The program prioritizes applicants who are underrepresented in the 
green jobs industry, such as women, LGBTQIA+ youth, and young people of 
color.
• Skills development:
 Beyond beekeeping, participants develop teamwork, leadership, and 
socio-emotional skills, empowering them to become future environmental 
leaders

 Mobilizes community members to advocate for policies on issues like 
immigrant rights, housing, and worker protections. This includes voter 
registration and education.

• Health Access:
 Assists community members with health insurance enrollment, SNAP (food 
benefits) enrollment, and provides health education and emergency food 
pantries.

 the Road New York focuses on supporting young people, including those 
who are college-bound, and offers opportunities for students to get 
involved through various programs and internships. College student programs and opportunities:
• Youth Power Project:
 This program empowers young people to become leaders and advocate for 
change. They focus on issues like educational justice, including 
improving school climate and ending punitive policies.
• College Access:
 The organization's Student Success Centers provide one-on-one and group
 guidance for the college application and financial aid process. Their 
model has a high college acceptance rate among participants and has been
 replicated across New York City.
• Internships:
 Make the Road New York offers internships, including legal internships,
 for students interested in mission-driven work. These programs provide 
hands-on experience in areas like advocacy, research, and project 
management.

 The organization was a key advocate for the New York Dream Act, which 
provides undocumented students with access to state financial aid for 
higher education.

long-standing initiative, established in 1989, and is funded by the New 
York State Education Department. Contributions to the community and college studentsFor the community

• Engages parents: It provides programming and support designed to increase parental involvement in their child's education.

 By fostering civic engagement and leadership skills in its 
participants, the program helps cultivate talented and motivated young 
people who will contribute to the community.

 as interns with the program. This provides valuable hands-on experience
 in program implementation and youth mentorship.

community service with local nonprofits, many of which are NPSI members.

 Organizations in the network, like Staten Island ACHIEVE, offer 
scholarships and mentoring to local students. Other member foundations 
also provide support for higher education.
