As a South Bronx-based organization, GWC serves immigrant communities 
and people of color by building, growing, and sustaining worker-owned 
green businesses. Its activities and impacts include:
• Creating local green businesses:
 Through its Co-op Academy, GWC has helped launch dozens of businesses, 
including those focused on solar panel installation, wellness, and 
culinary services.
• Fostering a democratic economy:
 By developing worker cooperatives, the organization promotes worker 
ownership and control, which builds local wealth and gives people a 
stake in their businesses.
• Promoting environmental justice: GWC's approach addresses environmental racism and seeks to create green jobs that are non-polluting and non-exploitative.
• Community education: It provides workshops, seminars, and training that use popular education methods to empower community members.
• Advocating for racial and gender equity: As a Black-led organization, GWC builds businesses that are rooted in principles of racial and gender equity. 
• What GWC does for college studentsWhile
 GWC does not have a program explicitly for college students, its Co-op 
Academy and other resources are open to aspiring entrepreneurs, 
including college students, who want to start a worker cooperative. GWC could be a valuable partner for college students through:
• The Co-op Academy: Students with a green business idea can receive hands-on training, coaching, and technical assistance to launch a cooperative.
• Applied learning:
 The program offers a practical, real-world application of classroom 
learning in social entrepreneurship, environmental justice, and business
 development.
• Networking: It connects participants with active cooperative businesses, experienced business developers, and other entrepreneurs
Civic Domains: Economic, Workforce, Labor, Employment, Finance, Business, Community Development
Capabilities / Expertise: Cooperative Business Development, Training and Coaching, Technical Assistance, Networking
Communities Served: Immigrant Communities, People of Color, College Students
INI Alignments: Environmental Justice, Racial Equity, Gender Equity

Notes / Insights:
The Thinkubator focuses on youth and workforce development in the Bronx,
 empowering young people by providing them with practical training, 
career opportunities, and a voice in local issues. Programs and initiatives for the community
• Workforce Development: Offers paid internships and work-based learning opportunities for young adults.
• Youth-led Consultancy:
 The "Thinkubator Solves" program employs young "Junior Consultants" to 
craft solutions for real-world problems facing businesses and 
organizations.
• Community Advocacy:
 The organization convenes youth, community leaders, and business 
leaders to address issues impacting the economic growth of the Bronx. It
 also directly supports community initiatives, such as providing meals 
and groceries to families.
• The Think Tank:
 Operates a research center that focuses on policy and action research 
to drive social change, especially for marginalized communities. 
• Engagement with college students
• Internships: Provides paid internships and work-based learning experiences specifically designed to connect college students to employers.
• Consultancy Projects:
 College students act as Junior Consultants in the "Thinkubator Solves" 
program, applying their skills to tackle real business challenges.
• Leadership and Mentoring: Connects students with professionals to provide mentoring and professional development.
• Talent Placement: Operates a Bronx-focused job board and employment matching service to help college students and other young adults find jobs.
Capabilities / Expertise: Practical Training, Career Opportunities, Voice in Local Issues, Paid Internships, Work-Based Learning, Junior Consultants, Policy and Action Research, Social Change, Mentoring, Professional Development, Talent Placement
Communities Served: Young People, Marginalized Communities, College Students, Employers, Community Leaders, Business Leaders, Families

Program/Org Affiliation: BCDI's board comprises various stakeholders from the Bronx
Notes / Insights:
The BCDI is a community-led economic development organization that 
focuses on building shared wealth and ownership for low-income people of
 color. The organization operates several projects that support the 
wider community and college students. Community-facing initiatives
• The BronXchange:
 A marketplace that connects Bronx institutions and nonprofits with 
local businesses to localize their purchasing and build community 
wealth.
• Bronx Innovation Factory (BXIF):
 A center for advanced manufacturing led by women and people of color. 
It focuses on shared wealth creation and developing community-based 
solutions using digital fabrication technology.
• Planning and Policy Lab: This lab creates and maintains the long-term strategy for equitable development and public policy in the Bronx.
• Economic Democracy Learning Center:
 A leadership institute that educates stakeholders on economic democracy
 principles and prepares them to lead future initiatives. 
• College students and youth
• Bronx Innovation Factory:
 College students can participate in programs focused on advanced 
manufacturing, digital fabrication, and entrepreneurship. The goal is to
 nurture the innovative capacity of Bronx residents and position 
entrepreneurship as a tool for economic security.
• Internships:
 BCDI offers internship opportunities, which provide valuable hands-on 
experience in community-driven economic development projects.
• Leadership development:
 BCDI's initiatives, such as the Economic Democracy Learning Center, aim
 to cultivate future community leaders, which can be beneficial for 
college students and other stakeholders interested in local development.
• Research collaboration:
 The organization has a history of collaborating with educational 
institutions like MIT's Community Innovators Lab (CoLab), which provides
 research and project support.
Capabilities / Expertise: Leadership Development, Research Collaboration, Internship Opportunities, Entrepreneurship, Digital Fabrication, Advanced Manufacturing, Strategic Planning, Policy Development
Communities Served: Low-Income People of Color, College Students, Youth, Bronx Residents

Program/Org Affiliation: founder and CEO of the Bronx Tech Hub
Notes / Insights:
The Bronx Tech Hub bridges the digital divide and promotes economic growth by offering several types of programming. For the community:
• Skills training:
 Offers programs for Bronx residents of all ages, including children 
learning STEM, professionals seeking jobs, and seniors receiving digital
 skills training.
• Entrepreneurship: Provides resources, mentorship, and networking to help underrepresented founders and residents build tech startups.
• Workforce development: Connects residents with tech opportunities and partners with other organizations to elevate the local economy. 
• For college students:
• Fellowships:
    ◦ AI Fellowship: Guides students toward software engineering or AI bootcamps.
    ◦ Climate Tech Fellowship:
 A paid fellowship for CUNY students that includes industry mentorship, 
professional development, and real-world exposure to the clean energy 
and climate technology sectors.
• Job fairs: Organizes events that connect students with potential employers for internships and full-time roles.
• Networking: Provides opportunities for students to connect with tech startups, nonprofits, and other tech professionals.
Capabilities / Expertise: Skills Training, Entrepreneurship Support, Workforce Development, Fellowship Program Management, Job Fair Organization, Networking
Communities Served: Bronx Residents, Children, Professionals, Seniors, College Students, CUNY Students

Program/Org Affiliation: corresponding secretary and primary email contact
Notes / Insights:
The BCEQ is an active 501(c)(3) organization involved in numerous local initiatives. Recent updates and activities include: 
• Ongoing Advocacy:
 They continue to take public stances on local issues, such as 
submitting testimony to the NYC Council in October 2023 regarding green 
infrastructure and climate change mitigation.
• Community Reports: In 2021, they completed an environmental justice research project focusing on health, wealth, and education in the Bronx.
• Recent Events: They have hosted events, including a "Report to the Community" conference in October 2023.
• Harlem River Working Group: The organization actively works to improve public access to the Harlem River and address related environmental concerns.
• Newsletters: BCEQ maintains a newsletter, The Current!, and sends action alerts to keep the community informed. 
• Contributions to the community and college studentsBCEQ's
 work is driven by community involvement and focuses on several key 
areas. The organization provides opportunities and benefits for 
community members and college students through: 
• Advocacy and Education:
 They promote clean air and water, and work to protect nature and 
historic heritage in the Bronx through environmental policy. They also 
raise awareness through symposiums and community outreach.
• Volunteer Activities: The BCEQ organizes hands-on conservation work, such as tree plantings, restoration activities, and beach clean-ups.
• Engaging Students:
 The organization has a history of involving college students, including
 events like a "Students Explore Bronx Waterways" initiative and hosting
 workshops at Manhattan College.
• Partnerships and Forums:
 By partnering with other groups like the Bronx River Alliance and 
holding events like the "Speak Up Candidates Forum," they provide 
platforms for community dialogue on environmental issues.
• Local Action: As an all-volunteer organization, the BCEQ mobilizes community members to act on local environmental problems.

Program/Org Affiliation: the Founder and Executive Director of The Bronx is Blooming (BiB)
Notes / Insights:
The Bronx is Blooming works to foster environmental stewardship and community development throughout the Bronx. Their main activities include: 
• Environmental stewardship:
 They train local youth to lead volunteer projects in Bronx parks. Since
 2011, they have stewarded over 20,000 trees across more than 20 parks.
• Civic engagement: They inspire residents to advocate for their local parks, driving change in their neighborhoods.
• Community building:
 They organize volunteer revitalization projects and create 
opportunities for community members to work together in their local 
green spaces. 
• How they help college studentsBiB offers opportunities for college students through its programs and internships. Specific ways they help include: 
• Paid internships and jobs:
 The organization provides seasonal job training and employment 
opportunities to young Bronx residents, which includes college-aged 
participants.
• Volunteer coordination: College students can volunteer at park revitalization projects, gaining hands-on environmental stewardship experience.
• Professional opportunities:
 The organization attracts college students and graduates to its staff. 
For example, Ava Chow, a Seasonal Environmental Program Coordinator, 
began as a Civic Corps member and is a graduate of Columbia University.
• Community connections:
 College students passionate about environmental justice and 
sustainability can gain valuable experience and connections through the 
organization's work.
Capabilities / Expertise: Environmental Stewardship, Civic Engagement, Community Building, Volunteer Coordination, Professional Opportunities
Communities Served: Youth, Residents of the Bronx, College Students

Notes / Insights:
The BCC Office of Workforce Development & Continuing Education 
offers non-degree educational programs for both the local community and 
college students to help them improve their career prospects and 
economic security. For the community:
• Adult basic education:
 The office offers classes for those needing to improve their English as
 a Second Language (ESL) or prepare for high school equivalency exams.
• Vocational training:
 They provide training and certificate programs for high-demand fields 
such as construction, healthcare, early childhood education, and 
technology.
• Job preparation and placement:
 For economically disadvantaged residents, the department provides 
services like job readiness skills training, job placement assistance, 
and career counseling.
• Training for special populations: The office has special programs for veterans and out-of-school youth. 
• For college students:
• "Stackable" credentials:
 The office collaborates with academic departments to develop 
credit-bearing vocational training programs. Students can earn an 
industry certification and often receive college credit for the work 
they complete.
• Career pathways:
 Students enrolled in workforce development courses receive guidance on 
career pathways, connecting their training to the college's degree 
programs.
• Low-cost training: Many courses are offered at low cost to help students with their careers
Capabilities / Expertise: Career Counseling, Job Placement Assistance, Job Readiness Skills Training, Low-Cost Training, Training and Certificate Programs
Communities Served: Economically Disadvantaged Residents, Veterans, Out-of-School Youth, College Students

Notes / Insights:
Staten Island Arts fulfills its mission to foster a diverse cultural community by providing a wide range of programs and resources. 
• Funding and grants: It offers project grants to artists, collectives, and non-profits who create arts projects on Staten Island.
• Community resource center:
 The organization's website features a "CultureWork" page with resources
 to assist cultural producers, including information on potential 
partners, venues, and marketing support.
• Cultural development:
 It serves as a local arts agency that supports community-based cultural
 development, having been founded by working artists and administrators 
in 1992.
• Racial and socioeconomic equity:
 The organization is committed to addressing systemic inequities through
 its programs and partnerships to help create a more inclusive cultural 
environment. 
• Support for college studentsStaten Island Arts offers services and programs that can benefit college students, particularly those studying arts or humanities.
• Internships:
 While current openings vary, the organization's mission to provide 
resources may include opportunities that lead to potential internships 
for college students. For example, the College of Staten Island (CSI) 
offers its own art and performing arts programs, with some potentially 
relevant coursework featuring internships.
• Grant opportunities: College students who are residents of Staten Island may apply for project grants to fund their creative work.
• Individual artist support:
 Students can also seek one-on-one guidance from staff like Deputy 
Director Gena Mimozo for assistance with their projects and ideas.
• Artist resources: The organization's online resources can help students find partners, venues, and other support for their own cultural projects.

Program/Org Affiliation: the Vice Chancellor for Career Engagement and Industry Partnerships

For college students
• Summer research program:
 BUEE offers a 10-week intensive summer research training experience for
 undergraduate students, with a focus on underrepresented students. 
Participants receive a stipend and housing.
• Research training:
 The program provides mentored research and training in urban ecology 
and environmental science, covering scientific communication, integrity,
 and quantitative literacy.
• Graduate program preparation: BUEE aims to prepare students for graduate programs in the urban ecology and environmental fields.
• Urban Sustainability Program (Brooklyn College):
 Students can choose from three tracks: Environmental Science, 
Environmental Economics and Business Management, or Environmental 
Sociology.
• Internship opportunities:
 Vice Chancellor Lauren Andersen leads CUNY-wide efforts to ensure 
postgraduate career success and facilitate industry partnerships. 
• For the community
• Community outreach:
 A key objective of the BUEE program is to involve students in promoting
 environmental awareness throughout the broader New York City community 
through partnerships.
• Community science:
 The Urban Ecology Center in Milwaukee offers a research and community 
science program, including sharing their data with students, 
researchers, and citizen scientists.
• Educational resources: Organizations like Urban Advantage NYC
 partner with community-based organizations to provide expert knowledge,
 resources, field trips, and data sets for schools and the public.
• Volunteer events: Numerous community-led events in Brooklyn support urban ecology, such as:
    ◦ Tree care: Caring for street trees in areas like Cobble Hill and with organizations like the Clean Bushwick Initiative.
    ◦ Park cleanups: Volunteer cleanups in local parks like Irving Square Park.
    ◦ Urban gardening: Events like "Help the Garden Day" at the Highland Park Community Garden and the Brooklyn Urban Gardener (BUG) certificate program.
    ◦ Special events: The Brooklyn College Tree Inventory event involves the public in mapping the campus forest.
Capabilities / Expertise: Research Training, Mentorship, Graduate Program Preparation, Internship Opportunities, Community Outreach, Community Science, Educational Resources, Volunteer Events

Program/Org Affiliation: Co-founder and Board Member of both South Bronx Unite
Notes / Insights:
The trust is focused on community-led development and anti-displacement 
strategies, advocating for community control of local land and assets. 
• Affordable housing:
 By separating ownership of the land from the buildings on it, the trust
 ensures that housing remains permanently affordable for residents, 
combating gentrification and displacement. In 2023, the trust announced 
plans to develop the Bronx's first permanently affordable cooperative 
housing in Belmont-Crotona.
• Green space and environmental justice:
 The trust is involved in creating public green spaces and addressing 
environmental injustices. It holds community planning sessions to 
develop the Mott Haven-Port Morris waterfront into a public space and 
combat air pollution, which is a major health issue in the area.
• Community organizing and advocacy:
 The trust empowers community members through workshops and advocacy 
campaigns to have a say in development decisions. It also organizes 
rallies and promotes legislation like the Community Land Act to benefit 
working-class New Yorkers.
• Arts and education:
 The organization seeks to create space for cultural, educational, and 
artistic venues that benefit the community. One project involved a 
collaborative effort to transform the vacant Lincoln Recovery Center 
into the "H.E. ARTs Community Center". 
• Support for college studentsThe trust and its partners offer opportunities and collaborations that involve college students:
• Academic partnerships:
 The trust partners with universities like Columbia, Pratt Institute, 
and City College of New York (CCNY) on research and collaborative 
projects.
• Research opportunities:
 As an example of a past collaboration, a studio course from the Hunter 
College Master of Urban Planning and Policy program worked with the 
Bronx Community Land Trust to address a planning problem.
• Curricular connections:
 Board member Monxo López, an educator at Hunter College, incorporates 
the trust's work into university courses and invites students to get 
involved with urban planning and organizing.
• Real-world experience:
 The work of the trust provides a real-world case study for students 
interested in urban planning, affordable housing, environmental justice,
 and community-led development.
Civic Domains: Community Development, Urban Planning, Affordable Housing, Environmental Justice
Communities Served: Low-Income Communities, Working-Class New Yorkers, College Students

Program/Org Affiliation: Director for Staten Island Women Who March
Notes / Insights:
SIWWM is a grassroots organization focused on progressive community engagement and social action.

 2018, it held a women's breakfast to inspire young women and connect 
female activists.

 SIWWM advocates for progressive values that reflect compassion and 
forward-thinking policy. It collaborates with diverse groups on Staten 
Island who share similar commitments to women's issues and social 
change.
• Voter registration and awareness: The group educates the community on elections and candidates, and has also promoted registering voters.
• Protests and political action:
 SIWWM organizes protests related to national events and encourages its 
members to take political action, such as contacting representatives.

 The organization has previously made an effort to engage with college 
students, including collaborating on events to inspire young women. The 
College of Staten Island (CSI) also has its own independent Women's 
Center, which is dedicated to creating an inclusive network for female 
students and encouraging community service.

ambassador programs.

Program/Org Affiliation: the Director of Communications & External Affairs
Notes / Insights:
The SIEDC works to create a thriving economy on Staten Island and 
improve the quality of life for its residents. Its community-focused 
activities include: 
• Promoting local investment:
 Since 1993, the SIEDC has attracted over $550 million in new 
investment, created more than 3,500 jobs, and helped develop over 1,000 
acres of vacant industrial land.
• Assisting small businesses:
 The organization provides services such as financing assistance, job 
training, technology assistance, and help navigating government 
regulations.
• Hosting community events:
 The SIEDC organizes public events throughout the year, such as the 
Annual Business Conference and the Health & Wellness Expo.
• Advocating for infrastructure:
 SIEDC staff and urban planners work to advance projects that benefit 
the borough, including the proposed West Shore Light Rail. 
• What the SIEDC does for college studentsThe
 SIEDC actively engages with college students, particularly those at the
 College of Staten Island (CSI). Benefits and opportunities for students
 include: 
• Business conference access:
 The SIEDC's Annual Business Conference offers students a chance to 
network with professionals, attend educational seminars, and learn from 
speakers in business, marketing, sustainability, and public policy.
• Career development:
 Students can gain valuable insights into different industries, with a 
focus on those that are relevant to Staten Island's economic growth, 
such as transportation, construction, and energy.
• Leadership programs:
 The conference also features the "20 Under 40" Leadership Awards, which
 spotlights young professionals and highlights the career pathways 
available to students.
• Partnerships with colleges:
 The SIEDC has partnered with the College of Staten Island to host 
events like the Transportation and Infrastructure Summit, which provides
 a platform for students to engage with leaders from public and private 
sectors.
Capabilities / Expertise: Community Engagement, Business Development, Career Development, Leadership Development, Infrastructure Advocacy

Notes / Insights:
CACF is the nation's only pan-Asian children and families' advocacy 
organization. Its work is primarily centered in New York and includes: 
• Policy and advocacy:
 CACF pushes for equitable access to funding, education, health care, 
and other support services for marginalized AAPI communities.
• Community support initiatives:
 The organization administers several funds, including the New York 
State AAPI Community Development Fund, to support AAPI-led and -serving 
community-based organizations across the state.
• Research: CACF conducts research to identify the needs of AAPI families and provides recommendations to address these issues.
• Capacity building: It helps organizations improve their ability to serve AAPI children and families.
• Coalition building: CACF brings together numerous community organizations to create a unified and powerful voice for the AAPI community. 
• What CACF does for college and high school studentsCACF has several programs specifically focused on youth leadership and development for high school and college students. 
• Asian American Student Advocacy Project (ASAP):
 This initiative helps AAPI high school students develop leadership 
skills to advocate for education issues affecting their communities.
• AAPI-LEAD:
 An annual youth conference series, it brings students together to learn
 about AAPI history, advocacy, and policymaking. In 2025, CACF expanded 
this program to Long Island for the first time.
• AAPI Youth Community Action Network (AYCAN): This network connects AAPI and allied teens from across the country for virtual discussions and service-learning projects.
• Healing Ourselves, Healing Our Communities:
 A program and workshop series designed for AAPI high school and college
 students, it promotes racial healing and supports mental health.
• Internships: CACF also offers summer internships for students, placing them within its policy, programs, and communications teams.
Capabilities / Expertise: Advocacy, Leadership Development, Capacity Building, Coalition Building, Research, Service-Learning, Mental Health Support
Communities Served: Asian American and Pacific Islander (AAPI) Communities, High School Students, College Students, Teens
INI Alignments: Education Equity, Youth Empowerment, Community Development

Notes / Insights:
The Break Free Foundation aims to break the stigma surrounding mental 
health and substance use disorders by providing advocacy, education, and
 support. Key community initiatives include: 
• Recovery Scholarships:
 The foundation provides financial aid for individuals seeking treatment
 for substance use or co-occurring disorders, which is funded through 
events like the biannual Recovery on the Runway fundraiser.
• Educational Initiatives:
 They organize events and collaborate with mental health activists to 
educate the public, increase understanding, and reduce stigma.
• Community Building:
 They host online and in-person events, including women-led and LGBTQ+ 
support groups, to create a supportive community for people in recovery.
• Advocacy through Art: Through their fashion shows, such as Break Free NYFW, they use art as a platform to raise awareness about mental health and recovery. 
• What they do for college studentsThe
 foundation does not appear to have specific programs designed for 
college students, but students could benefit from their broader 
initiatives: 
• Recovery Scholarships:
 College students can apply for the recovery scholarship to cover 
treatment costs if they are struggling with mental health or substance 
use disorders.
• Support Groups: Students can join the foundation's support groups to connect with others and find peer support.
• Volunteer Opportunities:
 The foundation offers volunteer opportunities for peer support groups 
and in-person events, which could be relevant experience for students.
Capabilities / Expertise: Advocacy, Education, Support, Fundraising, Event Organization, Community Building
Communities Served: Individuals in Recovery, LGBTQ+, Women, General Public, College Students

Notes / Insights:
The foundation heavily relies on volunteers to support its mission, 
particularly for its community garden, known as Nonno's Garden. 
• Nonno's Garden:
 Volunteers of all skill levels can help with planting, caring for, and 
harvesting vegetables and other produce. This program supports both the 
Belvedere Club's kitchen and a local Staten Island food pantry.
• Event support:
 As a non-profit organization that runs many public events, the 
foundation also needs volunteers for its festivals, cultural programs, 
and special functions.
    ◦ Internship opportunitiesThe
 Italian Cultural Foundation does offer internships to college students,
 as confirmed by social media posts where they have featured their 
summer interns. However, specific details about application procedures, 
departments, and deadlines are not listed on their website. For college students interested in internships, the recommended course of action is to:
• Network with other organizations:
 College students of Italian-American heritage can also look into other 
organizations like the Italian Language Foundation (ILF), which offers 
its own exclusive internships for college student members.
Getting involved with the Italian Cultural Foundation offers students valuable benefits beyond work experience:
• Community engagement: The experience allows students to connect with their local community and give back.
• Cultural immersion: It provides a hands-on way to learn about and preserve Italian heritage.
• Skill development: Students can gain practical experience in event planning, non-profit management, and gardening.
• Networking: It offers opportunities to network with professionals and other members of the Italian-American community
Capabilities / Expertise: Event Support, Gardening, Internship Opportunities, Networking, Non-Profit Management
Communities Served: College Students, Italian-American Community, Local Community

Program/Org Affiliation: NOAA-CREST partner (external research collaborator frequently linked with BCC Geospatial center)
Notes / Insights:
Dr.
 Reza Khanbilvardi is a prominent and highly active figure at the City 
College of New York (CCNY), not at BCC (Bronx Community College). 
He is a Professor of Environmental Engineering and Director of several 
initiatives focused on Earth system sciences and environmental 
engineering. His work profoundly impacts students and the wider 
community through his research, academic leadership, and mentorship. Contributions to students and the communityFor students
• Education and mentorship:
 As a professor and director of the Earth System Science & 
Environmental Engineering program at CCNY, Dr. Khanbilvardi provides 
students with a multidisciplinary education. His work covers topics such
 as water resources, remote sensing, and sustainability. In May 2025, he
 received a Longevity Award for his exceptional service and commitment 
to student mentorship.
• Research opportunities:
 He has been the principal or co-investigator on over $100 million in 
research grants. This funding provides students with hands-on research 
experience in fields like flash-flood monitoring and climate change 
impacts.
• Workforce development:
 Dr. Khanbilvardi founded NOAA Cooperative Science Centers (CREST and 
CESSRST) to train students for careers with agencies like the National 
Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA). 
• For the community
• Environmental solutions:
 His research focuses on real-world environmental problems, including 
water resources management and the impact of climate change. The 
findings from these projects can inform policy and help communities 
adapt to environmental challenges.
• Partnerships and outreach:
 Dr. Khanbilvardi has secured extensive grant funding from federal 
agencies (e.g., NASA, NSF, USAID) and the City of New York. This work 
benefits the broader community by addressing water and environmental 
concerns that affect the local region.
• UN collaboration:
 He is the Executive Co-Director of the UN University Hub at CCNY, which
 applies remote sensing technology to address challenges in urban 
sustainability and resilience. This work provides global and local 
benefits through technological innovation.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Research Leadership, Workforce Development, Partnerships and Outreach, UN Collaboration
Communities Served: Students, Community, Local Region, Global Community

Program/Org Affiliation: Director for Special Projects, Office of Academic Affairs (faculty/admin)
Notes / Insights:
As
 Director for Special Projects and the CUNY Research Scholars Program at
 Bronx Community College (BCC), Katherine Acevedo-Coppa supports 
students through direct mentorship and administrative oversight of key academic initiatives. Her work also extends to the wider community through her involvement in college governance and research promotion. For students
• Encourages undergraduate research:
 As director of the CUNY Research Scholars Program at BCC, Acevedo-Coppa
 mentors students and encourages their participation in undergraduate 
research projects, particularly in STEM fields. This program connects 
students with faculty mentors to conduct research over a year-long 
period.
• Showcases student achievements:
 She facilitates student attendance and presentations at academic 
conferences, providing valuable networking and public speaking 
opportunities. For example, in 2024, she led four BCC students to 
present at the Middle States Commission on Higher Education conference.
• Serves as an educator:
 Acevedo-Coppa is an adjunct lecturer in the Education and Academic 
Literacy Department at BCC. She teaches a variety of courses, both 
in-person and online, sharing her expertise with students directly.
• Fosters a supportive environment: Student reviews on https://www.ratemyprofessors.com/professor/2457863
 describe her as caring, engaging, and clear about her expectations. 
Former students praise her teaching style and willingness to help 
students succeed.
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Research Facilitation, Teaching, Student Support

Notes / Insights: Robert is the listed counselor/advisor for the College Discovery program and the College Discovery Student Leadership group. That program is explicitly designed to support underrepresented students with advising, leadership development, and mentoring — a ready-made pipeline for recruiting students into paid internships, civic-tech cohorts, or research-mentorship programs that VNGLE INI runs. Partnering with College Discovery means you can reach students who need wraparound support (stipend, advising, follow-through) rather than just one-off workshops.

professor
 Julia Miele Rodas is a Professor of English at Bronx Community College 
(BCC) who focuses on disability studies, using her courses and 
scholarship to benefit both students and the broader community. Her work centers on social justice, disability advocacy, and encouraging critical thinking. Contributions to students
• Encourages inclusive learning:
 In her teaching, Professor Rodas consciously works to accommodate 
different learning styles, cognitive approaches, and abilities to create
 an inclusive environment.
• Focuses on social justice issues:
 Her writing classes at BCC often use social justice themes as their 
focus, allowing students to explore complex issues like racism, mass 
incarceration, and the experiences of marginalized people.
• Fosters advocacy:
 Through course design, she empowers students to become "small-scale 
public disability advocates," teaching them how to integrate disability 
studies into their work and encouraging them to address social issues.
• Creates a "safe working laboratory":
 She cultivates a classroom environment where students can test new 
ideas about reading, writing, and culture without fear of failure, 
viewing learning as a "messy" but valuable process of asking bigger 
questions.
• Published student work: In 2024, she collaborated with BCC students on a chapter of a book, leading to their publication.
• Mentors student clubs:
 From 2008 to 2011, she served as the faculty advisor for the Bronx 
Community College Organization for Leadership in Disability (BOLD). 
• Contributions to the community
• Promotes disability studies:
 As a prominent disability studies scholar and Victorianist, her work 
helps to reshape how both academia and the public perceive disability. 
She is a founding co-chair of the CUNY Disability Scholars and co-chair 
of the Columbia University Seminar in Disability, Culture & Society.
• Leads museum initiatives:
 She has acted as a lead consultant for the Metropolitan Museum of Art's
 "Crip the Met" initiative, which focuses on making the institution more
 accessible.
• Organizes public events:
 As a faculty advisor for BOLD, she coordinated public events, such as a
 disability film screening and talkback, to raise awareness and educate 
the community.
• Reshapes perception of autism: Her book, Autistic Disturbances: Theorizing Autism Poetics from the DSM to Robinson Crusoe,
 challenges stereotypical and pathologizing views of autism. She 
provides a framework for understanding and appreciating autistic 
language within a broader literary context.
• Promotes dialogue through publications:
 Her numerous publications in literary and cultural disability studies 
have contributed to ongoing public and academic conversations about 
disability, identity, and rhetoric.
Capabilities / Expertise: Advocacy, Mentorship, Public Education, Community Engagement, Scholarship
Communities Served: Students, Academia, General Public, Justice-Impacted Individuals

Program/Org Affiliation: Director of Collaborative Education Programs (collaborative edu & partnerships)
Notes / Insights:
As
 Director of Collaborative Education at Bronx Community College (BCC), 
Eugene Adams helps students and the broader Bronx community through partnerships, pre-college enrichment, and international programs.
 A "possibility broker" with nearly 40 years of experience, he is also 
co-founder of the Male Empowerment Network (MEN). For his efforts, he 
was honored with a Presidential Volunteer Service Award. For BCC students
• Mentorship and guidance:
 Adams is known as a mentor and confidant for students, particularly 
West African immigrant students who often visit his office for advice on
 personal and academic issues.
• Male Empowerment Network (MEN):
 As a co-founder, Adams supports BCC's programs for male students. At a 
"Lunch with Leaders" event, he encouraged students to get involved with 
something bigger than themselves and become masters of more than one 
thing.
• Improved academic support:
 After attending an academic conference on Senegal, Adams gained a 
better understanding of how to create support programs tailored to the 
needs of BCC's West African student population.
• Academic preparation:
 Through the Male Empowerment Network (MEN) and other programs, Adams 
helps provide students with college credit and skill-building workshops 
in writing, science, mathematics, and critical thinking. 
• For the community
• Educational partnerships:
 Adams develops and strengthens educational partnerships throughout the 
Bronx with elementary, middle, and high schools. This includes 
pre-college programs that offer coursework and workshops to local middle
 and high school students.
• International programs: Adams is a strong advocate for international education and service-learning as a tool for empowering urban youth.
    ◦ He has coordinated programs for students in countries including Senegal and South Africa.
    ◦ He also serves as the U.S. Director of Operations for the Senegalese American Bilingual School in Dakar.
• Grant development:
 Adams has helped secure millions of dollars in grants from 
organizations like the U.S. Department of Education, Ford Foundation, 
and New York State. These grants have funded STEM and other educational 
initiatives for students both on and off campus.
• Community book donations:
 In 2024, Adams donated over 200 STEM-focused books to the Ghana Mobile 
Community Center project, an initiative run by his former student and 
mentee
Capabilities / Expertise: Mentorship, Partnership Development, Grant Writing, Program Development, Community Engagement
Communities Served: Students, West African Immigrant Students, Male Students, Local Middle and High School Students, Urban Youth

communities.

 Beyond his teaching role, Betancur is a freelance journalist who writes
 about Hispanic political identity and representation. This work helps 
to inform the public and amplify important community perspectives.
• Scholarly and creative work:
 He has published poetry and creative nonfiction, adding to the cultural
 and intellectual life of the college and the wider community. He was 
also featured in a Bronx Community College publication highlighting 
faculty achievements in research and publishing.

 While Betancur's direct involvement in community outreach is not 
explicitly detailed in search results, his role as an educator aligns 
with BCC's mission to promote civic engagement and social justice. The 
college as a whole is committed to ensuring students have opportunities 
for community involvement and public service.

Program/Org Affiliation: Associate Professor of Radiologic Technology and Chairperson of the Nursing and Allied Health Sciences Department
Notes / Insights:
As
 an Associate Professor of Radiologic Technology and Chairperson of the 
Nursing and Allied Health Sciences Department, Annette Ortiz supports 
students and the broader Bronx community through academic guidance, curriculum development, and preparation of a skilled local healthcare workforce. Contributions to students

 As Chairperson, Ortiz oversees critical health-related programs at BCC,
 including Radiologic Technology and Nursing. She appeared in a 2020 
YouTube video discussing the Radiologic Technology program and its 
benefits for students.
• Advising and mentoring:
 She serves as an academic advisor and an experienced mentor for

succeed.

 Based on her experience as a registered Radiologic Technologist in both

courses for the Radiologic Technology program.

• Skilled workforce development:
 Through her leadership of the Nursing and Allied Health Sciences 
programs, Ortiz helps develop local talent that provides critical 
healthcare services to the Bronx community.

 Faculty members like Ortiz support the college's wider mission to be 
responsive to community needs through partnerships with schools and 
community-based agencies. These collaborations help to attract local 
students to campus and connect families with the college's resources.
• Social justice initiatives:
 As a faculty member, Ortiz is part of a larger network of faculty, 
staff, and students who advocate for social justice within the college 
and broader Bronx community.

 Ortiz has been at BCC since 2010 and has served in multiple leadership 
roles, helping to shape the college's academic direction for over a 
decade

Notes / Insights:
Professor
 Stephen Powers at Bronx Community College (BCC) is a dedicated student 
advocate who contributes to both the student body and the community 
through teaching, mentoring, and administrative service. Contributions to students
• Student advocacy:
 As the college's ombudsperson since 2018, Powers intervenes on behalf 
of students to help them navigate complex college issues that might 
jeopardize their success.
• Academic support:
 He chairs the Committee on Academic Standing, which reviews and acts on
 student appeals regarding policies on grading, graduation requirements,
 and academic progress. He also advises students and trains other 
faculty on applying these policies fairly.
• Mentoring:
 Powers is known for building strong relationships with students and 
mentoring them, particularly those who have struggled academically. He 
focuses on helping them recover and succeed.
• Teaching:
 In the Department of Education and Academic Literacy, he teaches 
courses such as Introduction to Early Childhood Education and Urban 
Education. He also supervises student fieldwork and leads First-Year 
Seminars, which expose students to different fields of study.
• Grant writing:
 He helped secure a grant for his department to fund training in 
behavioral therapy and Google Classroom, providing students with 
additional skills and certifications. 
• Contributions to the community
• Generational learning:
 Having been at BCC since 2000, Powers has taught multiple generations 
of learners, including the children of former students. His work creates
 an enduring connection between the college and the surrounding 
community.
• Urban education research:
 Powers's research interests include mentoring in urban communities and 
the digital divide's effect on the achievement gap. This scholarship 
directly addresses issues facing the Bronx and other urban communities.
• Access to education:
 Drawing on his own experience as a first-generation college student, 
Powers is passionate about making higher education accessible. He seeks 
to provide the guidance and support to students that he once needed.
• CUNY-wide technology development:
 He serves on CUNY-wide committees on technology, which helps to shape 
the university system's approach to digital tools for teaching and 
learning. 
• Background and approach
• Extensive educational experience:
 Powers brings a wealth of experience to BCC, having previously worked 
as a middle school teacher, an elementary school principal, and a high 
school assistant principal.
• Reward in student success:
 He finds it most rewarding to see his students cross the stage at 
graduation, knowing that his work has helped them get their lives back 
on track and achieve a degree.
Capabilities / Expertise: Student Advocacy, Academic Support, Mentoring, Teaching, Grant Writing, Community Engagement
Communities Served: Students, First-Generation College Students, Urban Communities, The Bronx Community

 which provides high school students with an authentic, no-cost college 
experience. She also has more than 25 years of experience in pre-college
 programming, expanding higher education access for historically 
underserved students. For students: The College Now programAs director, Ms. Rivera oversees a program that specifically benefits New York City public high school students by providing:
• College-level courses: Qualified students can take free courses, getting a head start on their college journey.
• A seamless transition: The program eases the move from high school to college and better prepares students for a successful academic career.
• Access to campus life: Students can use BCC's facilities and participate in campus academic, social, and cultural events.

 Students interact with BCC faculty and students, and can receive 
assistance with accessing their CUNY accounts, email, and Blackboard. 
• For the community: Expanded access to higher educationMs. Rivera's work also provides a broader benefit to the community by:
• Strengthening dual enrollment:
 She participates in events, such as the Hispanic Education Summit, to 
discuss strategies for ensuring more equitable access to dual enrollment
 programs for students across New York State.
• Supporting underserved students:
 With her extensive experience, she is dedicated to increasing access to
 higher education for students who have historically been 
underrepresented.
• Fostering collaboration:
 As part of her advocacy for dual enrollment, she works to strengthen 
support networks and collaboration between CUNY and the New York City 
Department of Education.

 Ms. Rivera is specifically involved with the College Now program, it's 
worth noting that BCC offers many other services that benefit its

development services

 an Assistant Professor and Deputy Chairperson of the Engineering,

 As a full-time Automotive Instructor since 2007, Professor Patchoros 
teaches automotive courses and is highly regarded by his students for

ability to break down complex topics and his willingness to discuss 
vehicle-related matters.

 Professor Patchoros has helped to develop the automotive technology 
program's curriculum and ensure it meets industry standards. He has 
served on advisement committees and helped the program meet its NATEF

 As Deputy Chairperson of his department, he helps oversee academic 
programs and is the department's representative on the college's

 Professor Patchoros helps guide students in their academic journey. His
 industry experience and certifications allow him to provide valuable, 
real-world insight to students preparing for careers in the automotive 
field.

 By teaching and helping to accredit the automotive technology program, 
Professor Patchoros plays a key role in developing a skilled local

college. This experience ensures that BCC's curriculum remains relevant

 Professor and Chairperson of the Business and Information Systems

 He helped launch apprenticeship programs with major corporations like 
EY, where Computer Information Systems students gain valuable 
professional experience and are often hired after their apprenticeships.

• Serves as a campus leader:
 Throughout his 30-year tenure at BCC, Professor Clampman has served on 
many college committees, including the Middle States Steering Committee 
and the Strategic Planning Committee. His commitment to institutional 
service is integral to his persona.
• Helps build the local workforce:

Dr. Tyner-Mullings is a founding faculty member who joined Guttman in 
2011, drawn by the college's experimental model to improve outcomes for 
community college students. A sociologist with a background in 
alternative education, she has helped shape Guttman's unique, 
student-centric approach. Her work emphasizes:
• Alternative educational methods:
 Having researched successful alternative schooling models, like Central
 Park East Secondary School, she supports Guttman's project-based, 
interdisciplinary curriculum over traditional standardized testing.
• Embedded advisement:
 She advocates for integrating academic guidance directly into the 
student experience, an approach Guttman utilizes to foster closer 
student-faculty relationships.
• Experiential learning:
 Her work is guided by the philosophy that education should develop 
critical thinking skills that students can apply to a variety of 
real-world situations. 
• The Humanities and Social Sciences departmentAs
 chair, Dr. Tyner-Mullings leads a department that is integral to 
Guttman's core mission of student success. The department works with 
students and the wider community through several initiatives:
• Foundational curriculum:
 The department is responsible for core courses that help students 
develop the critical reading, writing, and research skills necessary for
 college success.
• Civic and community engagement:
 Guttman's model includes experiences intended to build civic 
engagement. The department contributes to these through coursework that 
encourages students to apply their classroom learning to community 
issues.
• Community partnerships:
 By focusing on urban issues, the department's work is directly relevant
 to New York City. Students and faculty collaborate with the community 
on academic and service projects.
• Research and pedagogy:
 Faculty in the department, including Dr. Tyner-Mullings, have published
 research on the high-impact practices and interdisciplinary teaching 
models used at Guttman. This work helps refine the college's educational
 model and contributes to the broader field of higher education reform.
